behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends entirely on the LLM's output quality. A mock LLM can be trained to return any response—including a generic one—and the test would pass. The real failure is: 'The LLM generates a generic response like Dear Sir/Madam, thank you for contacting us when given specific email content.' Only a real LLM call (or deterministic test endpoint with real-world coverage) will expose cases where the model fails to extract and reference specific points. Unit and Integration tests with mocked LLM responses cannot catch this failure because they do not test the LLM's actual semantic understanding.
behaviors.test_description: Call the draft generation step with a real email thread containing specific, unusual details (e.g., 'my grandmother's rare pottery collection'). Verify that the generated draft explicitly references these specific details, not just generic acknowledgments. Run this test 5+ times with the same input to confirm consistency.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: The consistency check likely uses semantic understanding (LLM-based or vector similarity) to detect contradictions, not simple string matching. A real failure is: 'The system fails to flag a semantic contradiction because the LLM-based checker missed the semantic relationship.' Mocking the KB would let you construct trivial contradictions that always get flagged. But with a real KB and real semantic matching, you discover edge cases where the checker fails—e.g., detecting that 'We have no office in Tokyo' contradicts 'Our Tokyo branch opened in 2020.' At Agentic level, this behavior requires testing the actual contradiction-detection mechanism with real data and real semantic inference.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a KB entry stating 'Company operates 3 North American locations.' Generate or provide a draft claiming 'We have expanded to 5 North American offices.' Run the consistency check and verify it flags this contradiction. Also test edge cases: negations ('We do NOT use X'), indirect contradictions ('We partner with Y' vs KB entry 'We compete with Y'), and synonyms.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: The failure is not about code logic (adding a disclaimer when KB returns empty); the failure is about LLM behavior: 'The LLM generates unsupported claims despite being told there is no relevant KB information.' A mocked LLM can be configured to always include a disclaimer. But the real LLM might confidently assert facts that have no basis, even when the code correctly passes empty KB results. Only an Agentic-level test with a real LLM will catch cases where the model fabricates information because it lacks grounding.
behaviors.test_description: Query the KB with a term that returns no results (use an intentionally obscure or non-existent entity). Generate a draft using the real LLM with explicit instructions that no KB entries were found. Parse the draft and verify: (1) a disclaimer or uncertainty marker is present ('I don't have information on...' or similar), and (2) the draft does not make specific factual claims about the entity. Run with multiple queries to catch variation.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: This is a workflow control behavior, not an LLM behavior. The failure is: 'A draft is sent without user approval.' While a simple Unit test might catch a missing approval check, a realistic Integration test catches more subtle failures: approval state not persisting between steps, race conditions in async workflows, or approval flags being reset unexpectedly. Integration level tests the actual workflow state machine with realistic sequencing—e.g., does approval status survive if the draft is edited? Is approval tied to the correct draft version? These failures only surface when state management is real, not mocked.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a test workflow: (1) call draft_generation(), (2) attempt send() without approval—verify email service is NOT called, (3) call approve(), (4) attempt send()—verify email service IS called. Use a real or in-process state store for approval status, not a mock. Verify that sending without approval fails at multiple checkpoints (before initial send attempt, after edits, after timeout).

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: This is the full agent pipeline with multiple agentic steps (draft generation, semantic consistency checking) and real infrastructure dependencies (email reading, LLM API, KB access). Individual Integration or Agentic tests might all pass in isolation: LLM generates good drafts, KB checking works, approval blocks correctly. But the Workflow fails because: data flows incorrectly between steps (draft text is truncated), LLM calls happen in wrong order (KB check before draft generation), or approval status is lost between pipeline steps. Only an end-to-end Workflow test with a real email thread and all components wired together catches these integration failures.
behaviors.test_description: Select a real email thread (or realistic fixture with multiple exchanges, specific questions, domain context). Execute the full pipeline: (1) read thread, (2) generate draft with real LLM, (3) consistency-check against real KB, (4) present approval prompt, (5) user approves. Verify end-to-end: (a) draft references the specific thread context, (b) no contradictions are flagged for correct facts and flagged for incorrect ones, (c) approval blocks send, (d) on approval, email is sent with correct content. Test with 2-3 different email domains and thread complexities.
